sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 2 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT, RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 51, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Clicking on the white block SWAPPED the centers! Left green now c center at (33-35, 9-11), right green W center at (30-32, 30-32). Left dark outline reappeared at (33-35, 12-14). Key discovery: white block is a swap button!
- Plan: Now right green has W center and can move. Try moving it RIGHT to see if it can reach the right panel/dark outline.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 50 actions; a strong score needs about 28 or fewer. You are 1.8x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
RIGHT didn't move the right green. The right green is at (30-32, 30-32) with W center. It's at the white block position. Earlier I found that the right green can only move LEFT/RIGHT when at the white block, but RIGHT doesn't work (blocked by purple bar?).
Let me reconsider. The right green is at the white block (30-32, 30-32). The purple bar is to the right of this position. So RIGHT would try to move into the purple bar, which blocks it.
What if I need to move the right green UP or DOWN first to find a gap in the purple bar? Earlier I tried UP and DOWN when the right green was at the white block and it didn't move. But maybe I need to move it LEFT first to get it out of the white block, then try UP/DOWN.
Let me try: move LEFT (to get right green out of white block), then try UP or DOWN.
Actually wait, I already know the right green can only move LEFT/RIGHT when at the white block. When I moved it LEFT earlier, it went to the corridor. Let me try moving it LEFT, then UP/DOWN.
But actually, I need to reconsider the whole puzzle. I've been going in circles for 50 actions. Let me think about what I know:
1. Two green squares with centers (W or c)
2. Two dark outlines (left and right)
3. A white block in the corridor
4. A purple bar separating left and right panels
5. The green with W center moves when directional keys are pressed
6. The green with c center doesn't move
7. Clicking on a green's center swaps the centers (when not inside absorbed dark outline)
8. Clicking on the white block swaps the centers
9. Moving a green into a dark outline absorbs it
10. Moving the green out releases the outline
What if the goal is to have BOTH dark outlines absorbed simultaneously? The left one can be absorbed by moving the left green into it. The right one... how can it be absorbed?
Wait, what if the right green CAN reach the right dark outline if I move it through a gap in the purple bar? Let me look at the purple bar more carefully.
The purple bar is at approximately rows 23-38, cols 33-36. The right green is at (30-32, 30-32). If I move the right green LEFT to the corridor, then UP or DOWN, maybe I can find a gap.
Actually, let me look at the board image. The purple bar appears to be a solid vertical bar. But there might be a gap at the top or bottom.
Let me try: move right green LEFT (to corridor), then UP repeatedly to see if there's a gap at the top of the purple bar.
Actually, I realize I need to be more strategic. Let me think about what the minimal solution could be.
If the goal is to absorb both dark outlines:
1. Left green inside left dark outline (absorbed) - I can do this
2. Right green inside right dark outline (absorbed) - I need to get the right green to the right panel
To get the right green to the right panel:
1. Right green needs W center to move
2. Right green needs to pass through the purple bar
What if the right green can pass through the purple bar at a specific row? Let me try moving the right green to different rows and then RIGHT.
Current state:
- Right green: (30-32, 30-32), W center
- Left green: (33-35, 9-11), c center
Let me try: move right green LEFT (to corridor), then UP to a different row, then RIGHT to see if it can pass through the purple bar at that row.
Actually, I already know the right green can only move LEFT/RIGHT when at the white block. When it's in the corridor, it can't move. So I need to move it LEFT from the white block, then somehow get it to move UP/DOWN.
Wait, I'm confused about the movement rules. Let me re-examine:
- When the right green is at the white block (30-32, 30-32) with W center, it can move LEFT/RIGHT
- When the right green is in the corridor (not at white block), it can't move
- When the left green has W center, it can move in all 4 directions (within the left panel)
So the right green can only move LEFT/RIGHT when at the white block. It can't move UP/DOWN from the white block. And it can't move at all when not at the white block.
This means the right green can never reach the right dark outline, because:
1. It can only move LEFT/RIGHT from the white block
2. RIGHT from the white block is blocked by the purple bar
3. It can't move UP/DOWN to find a gap
Unless... the right green can teleport or pass through the purple bar somehow.
Let me try a different approach. What if I need to click on the purple bar to open a gap? Or what if I need to click on the right dark outline to absorb it?
Actually, let me try clicking on the purple bar itself. Maybe it opens a passage.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: RIGHT doesn't move right green (blocked by purple bar). Right green at white block can only move LEFT/RIGHT. Key discovery: white block swaps centers. Need new approach to solve.
Plan: Try clicking on the purple bar to see if it opens a passage, or try moving right green LEFT then UP/DOWN to find a gap.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Current: right green W at (30-32, 30-32), left green c at (33-35, 9-11)
# Try clicking on purple bar to open a passage
# Purple bar is approximately at rows 23-38, cols 33-36
result = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 31, 'col': 35}])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("After clicking purple bar:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['pixels'] <= 15:
        print(f"  color={n['color']} px={n['pixels']} bound={n['boundary']}")
print("  level_completed:", result.get('level_completed'))
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
